title: Average, Median, Mode & Standard Deviation Calculator | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Average, Median, Mode & Standard Deviation Calculator
description: Calculate mean, median, mode, range, standard deviation, and variance from a set of numbers. Ideal for statistics, grades, and data analysis. Free, instant.
intro: This calculator returns **the 9 most commonly used descriptive statistics** for a set of numbers: **mean** (arithmetic average), **median** (middle value), **mode** (most frequent value), **standard deviation**, **variance**, **range**, **minimum**, **maximum**, and **sum**. Useful for **calculating grade point averages** for school or university, analyzing **salaries**, measuring **data dispersion** (variability in responses, response times, error rates), completing **descriptive statistics** assignments, and in general any exploratory analysis of a numeric dataset. Separate numbers with commas, spaces, or semicolons -- the calculator interprets them automatically. All calculations happen instantly in your browser (no data is sent anywhere).
keyTakeaway: **Mean** = sum/n (sensitive to outliers). **Median** = middle value (robust). **Mode** = most repeated. **Standard deviation** sigma = sqrt(variance) (how spread out data is). If mean and median are very different, there's **skew** in the distribution.
useCases: Calculate your overall GPA for school or university courses.
Analyze a dataset of salaries or prices (median vs mean).
Measure the spread of delivery times or response times.
Complete descriptive statistics homework for school or college.
Analyze results from an experiment or survey.
explanation:
## Measures of Central Tendency

These indicate **the typical value** or 'center' of a dataset.

### Mean (Arithmetic Average) -- mu or x-bar

```
mean = (x1 + x2 + ... + xn) / n
```

Sum of all values divided by the count. **Very sensitive to extreme values (outliers)**: if in a group of 10 people the average salary is $80,000 but one person earns $5,000,000, the average skyrockets and doesn't represent reality well.

### Median

The value that sits **in the middle** when data is sorted from smallest to largest.
- **Odd count**: the middle value directly.
- **Even count**: average of the two middle values.

**More robust than the mean**: not affected by extreme values. If the average salary says $80,000 but the median says $55,000, there are a few very high salaries skewing the mean.

### Mode

The **most frequently repeated** value in the dataset.
- Can have one (unimodal).
- Two (bimodal).
- Several (multimodal).
- None (all values are different).

The only measure that also works for **categorical data** (most common color, favorite flavor).

## Measures of Dispersion

These indicate **how spread out the data is from the center**.

### Variance -- sigma squared

```
variance = SUM((xi - mean)^2) / n
```

Average of the squared distances from the mean. Units are **squared** (if data is in dollars, variance is in dollars-squared).

### Standard Deviation -- sigma

```
std_dev = sqrt(variance)
```

Square root of the variance. Indicates **how far values typically deviate from the mean**. Same units as the original data (which is why it's used more than variance).

### Range

```
Range = maximum - minimum
```

Difference between the highest and lowest values. **Very simple but sensitive to outliers**.

### Coefficient of Variation (CV)

```
CV = std_dev / mean
```

Standard deviation divided by the mean. **Unitless**, useful for comparing dispersion between datasets of different scales. CV > 0.3 typically indicates **high relative variability**.

## When Mean and Median Disagree

| Scenario | Mean | Median | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mean >> Median | High | Lower | **Positive skew** (a few very high values pull the mean up). Example: salaries, home prices. |
| Mean << Median | Low | Higher | **Negative skew** (a few very low values pull the mean down). Example: age at death. |
| Mean = Median | Same | Same | **Symmetric distribution** (normal/bell curve). |

## Population vs Sample Standard Deviation

- **Population** (divides by n): `sigma = sqrt(SUM((xi - mean)^2) / n)`. Used when you have **all** the data.
- **Sample** (divides by n-1): `s = sqrt(SUM((xi - mean)^2) / (n-1))`. Used when data is a **sample** from a larger population and you want to **infer** population properties. The Bessel correction (n-1) adjusts for bias.

This calculator uses **population** standard deviation. For large n, the difference is negligible.

## The 68-95-99.7 Rule (Normal Distribution)

For normally distributed data:
- **68%** of values fall within +/-1 standard deviation of the mean
- **95%** fall within +/-2 standard deviations
- **99.7%** fall within +/-3 standard deviations

A value more than 3 standard deviations from the mean is typically considered an **outlier**.

## Common Mistakes

1. **Using the mean with outliers**: in skewed distributions (salaries, prices, response times), the mean is misleading. Use the median.
2. **Confusing mode with frequency**: the mode is the **value** that appears most often, not the count of how many times it appears.
3. **Averaging averages**: if quiz 1 has a mean of 70 (with 2 questions) and quiz 2 has a mean of 90 (with 10 questions), the overall mean is NOT 80 -- it's **weighted** by question count: `(70x2 + 90x10)/12 = 86.7`.
4. **Interpreting standard deviation without context**: a std dev of 5 can be high or low depending on the values. Use the coefficient of variation to compare.
5. **Calculating variance without squaring**: `SUM(xi - mean)` always equals zero. You must square the differences before summing.

faq.q: When should I use median instead of mean?
faq.a: When there are **extreme values that distort the picture** (salaries, housing prices, response times, stock returns). The median is more 'typical' in **skewed distributions**. Example: if 9 out of 10 people earn between $40K and $70K and one earns $5M, the mean is $540K but the median is $55K -- the median describes the group better. For symmetric distributions (normal/bell curve), mean and median are equal.

faq.q: What counts as a 'high' standard deviation?
faq.a: **It depends on the scale of the data**. A useful rule: if `std_dev / mean` (coefficient of variation) > **0.3**, the sample is heterogeneous. Low std dev = consistent data, little variability. High std dev = spread out data. In a class with mean grade 85 and std dev 3, grades are homogeneous. With std dev 15, there's a wide range of performance.

faq.q: How do I calculate my weighted GPA?
faq.a: **Multiply each grade by the course credits**, sum everything, and divide by the total credits: `weighted_mean = SUM(grade x credits) / SUM(credits)`. Example: Math (grade 88, 4 credits) + Physics (grade 75, 3 credits) = `(88x4 + 75x3) / (4+3) = 577/7 = 82.4`. This is different from the simple average of 81.5.

faq.q: What is the difference between population and sample standard deviation?
faq.a: **Population** (sigma) divides by `n` and is used when you have **all data** in the population. **Sample** (s) divides by `n-1` (Bessel's correction) and is used when you have a **sample** from a larger population for inference. For `n > 30` the difference is negligible. This calculator uses population standard deviation. For hypothesis testing and confidence intervals, use sample standard deviation.

faq.q: Can there be more than one mode?
faq.a: **Yes**. If two or more values appear the **same maximum number of times**, the dataset is **bimodal** (2 modes), **trimodal** (3), or **multimodal**. Example: `[2, 3, 3, 5, 7, 7, 9]` has modes 3 and 7. If all values are unique (no repetitions), there is no mode.

The famous **bell curve**. A symmetric distribution where mean = median = mode. The **68-95-99.7 rule** says: 68% of data falls within +/-1 std dev of the mean, 95% within +/-2 std dev, 99.7% within +/-3 std dev. It models many natural phenomena (heights, weights, measurement errors, IQ scores). It's the foundation of statistical tests (t-test, z-test).

faq.q: How do I detect an outlier in a dataset?
faq.a: **Classic rule**: a value is an outlier if it's beyond `mean +/- 3 x std_dev` (more than 3 standard deviations out). **Quartile method** (more robust): a value is an outlier if it falls outside `[Q1 - 1.5 x IQR, Q3 + 1.5 x IQR]` where IQR = Q3 - Q1. The second method is more robust because it doesn't use the mean (which is already affected by the outlier).

faq.q: What does it mean when median is greater than mean?
faq.a: It indicates **negative skew** (left-tailed distribution). There are a few very low values pulling the mean down. Example: age at death -- most people die between 70-85 (high median), but early mortality cases lower the mean. If median < mean, it's **positive skew** (right-tailed): the case with salaries, prices, etc.

faq.q: How is the median calculated for an even number of values?
faq.a: If `n` is **even**, the median is the **average of the two middle values** (positions `n/2` and `n/2 + 1` after sorting). Example with 8 sorted values: `2, 4, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13, 15`. Middle values: position 4 (value 7) and position 5 (value 9). Median = `(7 + 9) / 2 = 8`. If there were 7 values, the median would be the one at position 4.

example.title: Example: Analyzing test scores from a semester
example.result: Mean **81.1**, median 85, standard deviation **12.2** (moderate spread -- most scores cluster between 69 and 93).
